title: Welcome to Hindi: Vowels and Greetings
learningGoal: Learn the foundational Hindi vowels (Swar) and the most important daily greetings.
instructions: Welcome to your first Hindi lesson! In this block, we will introduce the Devanagari script, starting with the vowels (Swar). We will also learn how to greet people politely in Hindi. Take your time to read the sounds out loud.

lessonContent.bodyText:
Hindi is written in a script called Devanagari (देवनागरी). Unlike English, which is an alphabet, Devanagari is an 'abugida' (or alphasyllabary). This means every consonant has an inherent 'a' sound unless changed by a vowel mark. 

We read and write Devanagari from left to right. One of its most distinctive features is the horizontal line drawn across the top of the letters, called the 'Shirekha'. In this lesson, we will focus on the independent vowels, called 'Swar' (स्वर).

Hindi has 11 primary vowels. Let's learn them in pairs: short and long sounds. A horizontal line sits on top of all of them.

- अ (a) : Like 'a' in 'about'.
- आ (aa) : Like 'a' in 'car'.
- इ (i) : Like 'i' in 'sit'.
- ई (ee) : Like 'ee' in 'see'.
- उ (u) : Like 'u' in 'put'.
- ऊ (oo) : Like 'oo' in 'moon'.
- ए (e) : Like 'a' in 'say'.
- ऐ (ai) : Like 'a' in 'cat' (or 'ai' in some accents).
- ओ (o) : Like 'o' in 'go'.
- औ (au) : Like 'ou' in 'sound'.
- ऋ (ri) : A special vowel, sounds like 'ri'.

The word 'Namaste' (नमस्ते) is the most universal greeting in Hindi. You can use it to say 'Hello', 'Good morning', 'Good afternoon', or even 'Goodbye'. 

When saying it, it is polite to bring your hands together in front of your chest and bow your head slightly, especially when greeting elders or strangers.

After saying Namaste, you usually ask how the other person is doing. The formal and most respectful way to ask 'How are you?' is 'Aap kaise hain?' (आप कैसे हैं?).

- 'Aap' (आप) means 'You' (formal/respectful).
- 'kaise' (कैसे) means 'how'.
- 'hain' (हैं) means 'are'.

learningObjective: The learner will be able to recognize Hindi vowels and politely greet a native speaker.

examples.targetText:
राहुल: नमस्ते। आप कैसे हैं?
अंजलि: नमस्ते। मैं ठीक हूँ।
examples.transliteration:
Rahul: Namaste. Aap kaise hain?
Anjali: Namaste. Main theek hoon.
examples.baseTranslation:
Rahul: Hello. How are you?
Anjali: Hello. I am fine.
examples.explanation: This is a standard, polite exchange between two adults meeting each other.

importantRules.ruleText: Always use 'Aap' (आप) when speaking to someone older, a stranger, or in a formal setting.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: We will learn informal pronouns later, but 'Aap' is the safest choice for beginners.

importantRules.ruleText: The horizontal line (Shirekha) must cover the entire word in Hindi.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Some letters have a broken line on top, but generally, the line connects the whole word.

cultureContext.contextText: In India, respect for elders and guests is deeply ingrained in the culture. Saying 'Namaste' with folded hands is considered highly respectful. Unlike English where 'Hello' is just a sound, 'Namaste' translates roughly to 'I bow to the divine in you'.
dosList: Do use 'Namaste' with folded hands when greeting an elder person.
dontsList: Don't use overly casual English greetings like 'Hey' with older relatives or formal acquaintances in a Hindi-speaking environment.

summary: You have taken your first step! You learned the Devanagari vowels (like अ, आ, इ, ई) and how to formally say 'Hello, how are you?' (Namaste, aap kaise hain?).

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What does the Hindi word 'Namaste' (नमस्ते) mean in English?
How do you formally say 'You' in Hindi?
Which Hindi vowel sounds like the 'a' in 'car'?

keyTakeaways: Hindi is written in Devanagari script from left to right.
Vowels are called 'Swar' and have short/long pairs.
Aap kaise hain? is the formal way to ask How are you.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Foundation of Hindi script (vowels) and basic respectful greetings.
importantFacts: The horizontal line on top of Hindi words is called the Shirekha.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners should recognize vowel shapes and be able to pronounce 'Namaste, aap kaise hain'.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great job! You have grasped the very basics of Hindi script and culture.
feedbackRules.onFail: Please review the vowels section carefully. Ensure you understand the difference between 'a' (अ) and 'aa' (आ).
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start! Keep practicing the pronunciation of the vowels out loud.

audioAssets.audioTranscript: अ आ इ ई उ ऊ ए ऐ ओ औ नमस्ते आप कैसे हैं?

hintText: Read the transliteration carefully, but try to look at the Hindi letters (अ, आ) to memorize their shapes.
commonMistakes: Pronouncing 'अ' (a) like the 'a' in 'apple'. It sounds like the 'a' in 'about'.
